findings.title: NACE mismatch: consulting firm winning construction contracts
evidence: Company NACE code 70220 = business consulting (Poradenske cinnosti v podnikani)
All 5 contracts are for construction/renovation work: gym construction, senior care building, school accessibility, university building safety
4 out of 5 contracts flagged as nace_mismatch in red_flags table
Contract extractions confirm service_category = construction_renovation for all extracted contracts
findings.reasoning: A business consulting firm with 1 employee should not be performing large-scale construction. This is not a marginal mismatch (e.g., IT consulting doing software) but a complete category disconnect. The firm may be acting as an intermediary/front for actual construction subcontractors.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Some consulting firms do project management for construction. However, these are 'zmluva o dielo' (works contracts) for actual construction, not project management. The contract subjects explicitly reference 'stavebne prace' (construction works) and 'stavebna cast' (construction part). Two contracts mention subcontracting is allowed, suggesting the firm itself does not perform the work.

findings.title: Micro supplier winning disproportionately large contracts
evidence: Company has 1 employee per RUZ registry
Equity of 51,647 EUR vs. total contract value of 892,261 EUR (17.3x leverage)
Single largest contract (467K) is 9x the company's equity
3 contracts flagged as micro_supplier_large_contract
Three concurrent large construction projects in different cities (Bratislava, Skalite, Zilina) within a 2-month window
findings.reasoning: A 1-employee consulting firm cannot physically execute 892K in construction work across multiple cities simultaneously. The 2024 financials show 963K revenue and 72.71% debt ratio, meaning the firm does process significant money flows, but with only 31K net profit on 963K revenue, the margin is thin — consistent with a pass-through entity that subcontracts everything and retains a small margin.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Some construction firms use subcontractors extensively and keep minimal staff. However, the combination of (a) consulting NACE code, (b) 1 employee, (c) registered at director's home address, and (d) simultaneous multi-city projects makes this unlikely to be a genuine construction operation.

findings.title: Potential contract splitting at Ekonomicka univerzita
evidence: Two separate contracts with same buyer within 2 months: 467,641 EUR (Jan 16) + 157,610 EUR (Mar 10) = 625,252 EUR combined
First contract uses UVO section 110 (below-threshold procedure)
Second contract uses section 108 (low-value contract exemption, no UVO reference)
Both are construction/renovation at same institution
YEMANITY is the 3rd largest supplier to these buyers since 2025
findings.reasoning: The combined value of 625K for the same buyer in 2 months raises the question of whether these should have been procured together under a more competitive procedure. The section 108 exemption on the second contract (157K) avoids full procurement. However, the projects are at different buildings (gym vs. V1/V2 teaching buildings) and have different purposes (new gym build vs. security upgrade), which could legitimately justify separate procurements.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Different buildings and different project scopes can legitimately be procured separately. The university may have received separate budget allocations. Without knowing the full procurement documentation, splitting cannot be confirmed.

findings.title: Hidden price on Skalite addendum
evidence: Contract 12075436 (Dodatok k zmluve, Obec Skalite) has suma = NULL (0 EUR displayed)
Flagged as hidden_price in red_flags
The addendum references Plan obnovy (EU Recovery Plan) compliance and recycled materials
findings.reasoning: A zero-value addendum that changes material specifications and adds EU Recovery Plan compliance requirements may not need a price change if it only modifies technical specifications. However, changing to recycled materials could affect costs.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Addenda that modify technical specifications without changing the contract price are common. The original contract 12036751 already has the full 266K value. This is likely a technical amendment, not price concealment.

findings.title: Signatory overlap flags are buyer-side, not supplier-side
evidence: Ferdinand Dano (rector of Ekonomicka univerzita) signs for 15 different suppliers — he is the buyer representative
Peter Fiabane (primator of Zilina) signs for 24 different suppliers — he is the buyer representative
Jozef Cech (starosta of Skalite) signs for 11 different suppliers — he is the buyer representative
All three are public officials signing contracts on behalf of their institutions with various suppliers
findings.reasoning: The signatory_overlap flags in this case are false positives. They detect that a person signs contracts with many different suppliers, but these are all buyer-side officials (rector, mayor, village head) who naturally sign all their institution's contracts. This does not indicate any irregularity with YEMANITY specifically.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: This IS the innocent explanation. Public officials sign contracts as part of their statutory duties.

findings.title: Young company rapidly winning government contracts
evidence: Company founded 2022-11-16 (age ~3 years at time of contracts)
Second shareholder Filip Sobol joined only 2024-03-19 (less than 2 years before contracts)
Historical shareholder Tomas Polka departed (ownership change)
No prior CRZ contract history before January 2026
First CRZ contract immediately at 467K EUR
Revenue of 963K in 2024 suggests prior non-CRZ activity
findings.reasoning: The firm went from zero government contracts to 892K in under 3 months. However, the 2024 revenue of 963K means the firm was already doing significant business outside CRZ (possibly private sector or as subcontractor). The ownership change (Polka out, Sobol in) shortly before the government contract spree could indicate restructuring for procurement eligibility.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Companies can legitimately build private sector experience before entering government procurement. The 2024 revenue shows the firm was active. However, the NACE mismatch undermines this — consulting revenue of 963K is plausible, but then pivoting to construction is not.

findings.title: Missing contract expiry dates on all contracts
evidence: All 5 contracts flagged with missing_expiry
platnost_do field is empty for all contracts
findings.reasoning: Construction contracts (zmluva o dielo) typically specify completion deadlines within the contract text rather than in the CRZ metadata field. The extraction confirms 'fixed_term' duration for the contracts. This is a data-quality issue in CRZ metadata, not a substantive concern.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: This is standard for construction contracts in CRZ. Dismissed as systemic metadata gap.

findings.title: Subcontracting mentioned but no subcontractors named
evidence: Contracts 12036751 and 12088132 have subcontracting_mentioned = 1
Both have subcontractor_count = 0 (no named subcontractors)
Company has 1 employee and consulting NACE code
findings.reasoning: The contracts allow subcontracting but do not name specific subcontractors. For a 1-employee consulting firm doing construction work, the actual builders are likely subcontractors — but their identities are not disclosed in the contract text. This reduces transparency and makes it impossible to verify who actually performs the work and whether they have appropriate qualifications.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Slovak procurement law does not always require naming subcontractors at contract signing. They may be disclosed later during execution. However, combined with the NACE mismatch and micro-supplier flags, unnamed subcontracting raises the question of whether YEMANITY is primarily a pass-through entity.

findings.title: No cross-company network connections found
evidence: foaf.sk shallow scan found 0 cross-supplier connections
Jan Sutiak and Filip Sobol appear only in YEMANITY s.r.o.
No shared persons with other CRZ suppliers
No RPVS (beneficial ownership register) data available
findings.reasoning: The network scan did not reveal hidden corporate connections. However, this was a shallow scan of a single supplier. The historical shareholder Tomas Polka (address: Zilina) could have connections not captured in this scan. The absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Many legitimate small businesses have clean corporate networks. The lack of connections is neutral.

findings.title: Geographic mismatch: rural Cadca-district firm serving Bratislava institutions
evidence: Company registered in Nova Bystrica (Cadca district, northern Slovakia, near Polish border)
Two highest-value contracts are for Ekonomicka univerzita in Bratislava (~300km away)
Company address is director Jan Sutiak's home address
findings.reasoning: A home-registered consulting firm from a remote village winning construction contracts in the capital is unusual but not impossible. Construction firms sometimes operate nationally. However, combined with other flags, it adds to the pattern of a firm that exists primarily on paper at the director's home.
findings.innocent_explanation_considered: Many Slovak construction firms are registered outside major cities and work nationally. The Skalite contract is actually close to Nova Bystrica (same district), which makes geographic sense for at least that project.

risk_assessment.primary_concern: YEMANITY s.r.o. appears to operate as a pass-through entity: a 1-employee business consulting firm registered at a director's home in rural Slovakia, winning 892K EUR in construction contracts across multiple cities within 2 months. The NACE mismatch, micro-supplier flags, and unnamed subcontracting collectively suggest the firm intermediates government construction contracts rather than performing the work itself.
recommended_next_steps: Deep investigation of UVO procurement records (tenders 544941 and 548246) to check number of bidders, competitor identities, and evaluation criteria
RPVS beneficial ownership lookup for YEMANITY s.r.o. to check if UBO registration exists
Investigate Tomas Polka (historical shareholder) for connections to construction firms or public officials
Verify whether YEMANITY has construction trade licenses (zivnostenske opravnenie) despite consulting NACE code
Check Jan Sutiak and Filip Sobol for personal connections to officials at Ekonomicka univerzita or Obec Skalite
Request the original Mesto Zilina contract 802/2025 which is not in the CRZ database
